item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the anomaly is and what it does isn't particularly interesting to me. What I absolutely love about the article, however, is how the transcript reads like it really was translated from German! The inclusion of the original spelling in the indecipherable audio is something I've never seen done in a piece of writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think CupertinoEffect is still active…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In stage 5 you wrote

sections of the brain begin to emit heats measured up to

I think that "heats" should be singular in that sentence and using the word measuring rather than measured might sound better. Check a reliable source for grammar rules which I am not.
I looked up the meaning of Glomar Response. Learning that it means neither confirm nor deny was quite amusing.
Finally a note about the discovery log,
Is a gas leak the best cover story for this scp. Considering the heat produced by the brain fragments, I thought a fire in the breakroom would be a more natural fit. The foundation could say the tv was destroyed and then they don't even have to replace it. The bodies could be replaced with other corpses burned beyond recognition so the families can still have closed casket funerals for their loved ones. A gas leak would only explain why people passed out and died. It wouldn't explain why there skulls burnt from the inside out. That's why I think a fire would be a better cover story.

item.title: Reviewer Spotlight
item.content:encoded: Roughly the melting point of tin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
„ heats measured up to 520 K (467°F)”

I still don’t know how much that is!

item.title: Re: Question
item.content:encoded: That right there is a collapsible. You can usually hover over and click on it, and it will reveal more text.

item.title: Question
item.content:encoded: How did you make it so the + Document showed more? is it a tool or something?

item.title: Reviewer Spotlight
item.content:encoded: I chose this article because it was the first draft that I critiqued that was successfully posted and because it remains (IMO) seriously underrated.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source this image is from indicates it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: error,
item.content:encoded: it says on one of the lines that "-An internal device that emits a large amounts of type-3 anomalous radiation1 while active," Fix the part that says "a large amounts"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you. I think?
What's the cute part?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is kinda cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That this is only at +10 boggles me. Seriously underrated, IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Phew, glad i'm not seeing things that aren't there!
In fact, that I was considering that probably means you've done a really good job of disguising that prompt in the rest of your work. That's a really cool way of getting an idea for an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep, someone noticed!
For this one I started off with taking that phrase and then trying to inch it away as far as I could to get something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the general strangeness of a broken piece of whacky early 20th century neuroscience.
What did it for me might have been something I was reading into this that wasn't intended. Were you meaning to have this be a little bit of a joke about TV rotting your brain?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: how did I avoid this article? I recall reviewing it in the drafts and it's nice to see it's even better on the mainsite!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dials are disconnected. Like from all the internal components. I did say they were "removed" initially, but I changed it so I could have the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the writing on this is for the most part fine, the effect and backstory and all that just felt sort of uninspired, and there wasn't anything about it that really held my interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always like SCPs that have incomplete/not-intended effects, and this one does that particular idea pretty well. My only real complaint is that the picture doesn't match the description. You say that all the dials have been removed, but I can clearly see at least two of them in the picture. That isn't preventing me from upvoting, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the guides said that even though technically "amnesiac" refers to someone suffering from amnesia, on the site it can be interchanged with amnestic.
I can't find which guide I read that in though so you'll have to take my word for it I suppose.
That said it would take me all of about ten seconds to change the two times that I used amnesiacs to amnestics, so I may as well. Consider it edited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figured it would be O5 command's job to evaluate what to do with it in that case.

I'd say in some cases, yes. Though therein lies the conundrum, which is a question that I really like thinking about in terms of this article; if it's fixed, does it act properly, or will it act stronger than it does?

In my view, I'd think O5 would review stuff if it'd be a more serious concern. As it is, it's a concern, but one a site director could probably make and be reasonably informed about. Now, if this thing could cause the end of the world or risk an information breach, I'd see this going to O5 council for approval. I'm not saying this is a big issue, but it seemed weird enough for me to comment on.

why thank you :3 my old avatar before this was koizumi, my fav

EDIT: Also, it's "amnestics", not "amnesiacs".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If a broken object is made to work as intended, it's probably easier to research than if it's broken.
If they risk destroying an SCP object, someone would have to evaluate the risks and the potential information that trying to fix it could yield. If they understand how it works to some degree, a site director is probably good enough. If they don't understand it very well at all, then it's probably not going to fly to eff with it for the risk of breaking it further ("secure contain protect" and whatnot).
I figured it would be O5 command's job to evaluate what to do with it in that case.
Of course, maybe I'm wrong.

I appreciate a no-vote over a downvote. It's a small victory. Or at the very least the evasion of a potential small failure.

Also yes Nanami and Komaeda are adorable. You have a nice Misato there yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I'm not a fan of this overall, it's been cleaned up enough and weird enough to my sensibilities that I'm no-voting instead of downvoting. One issue:

Research into SCP-2656 repairs is pending O5 approval.
Approval denied.

It feels very unlikely that the Foundation would repair this and fuck with it more. Even suggesting it feels like it's unlikely, so I can't see why it'd be suggested in the first place and brought up all the way to the O5 council for approval.

also is that nanami and komaeda in your avatar how cute omg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well that got out of hand.

I'm going to leave the temperatures as they are, at least for now. Kelvin seems more appropriate in this setting than Celsius, and I think having two measurements reads a little nicer. Readers who aren't familiar with K (which honestly I have to convert to Celsius in my head to get a feel for if a figure is "hot" or not) would probably appreciate that the °F stay where it is.
The temperature doesn't really matter that much anyway. It's just an arbitrary big number that's higher than the temperature at which human flesh burns. The notation doesn't have that much sway on the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Moved to Metric System thread)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I do have concerns about the quick dismissal of degrees Celsius as not a "real SI unit"

I think it is fair to say that it is a historical SI base unit that is retained because of thermometers and general usage. It still can't be defined only in terms of base units (which is the technical definition of a derived unit) and that pesky "degrees" keeps you from using with the SI prefixes (which is part of the technical definition of an SI unit).

but since I think we fundamentally agree more then disagree on how units should be approached for SCP Foundation fiction, I won't worry the point more then I already have.

Yes absolutely! I am a total pedant and tend to get lost in the weeds. Yes, technically degrees Fahrenheit are just as metric as degrees Celsius and technically degrees Celsius fails to meet the definition of an SI unit anymore, but in common use the latter is part of the "metric system" and the former is not. I do think that the difference between US Customary and Imperial is important (it is actually significant for many measurements) and I would rather people learn not to confuse them.

TL;DR: Use either K or °C and not both. Never use °F, °De, or °R.

To keep the discussion thread focused on the SCP, this conversation has been moved to the open forums: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1231713/systems-of-units

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this is getting far more convoluted than was necessary to begin with.

Also, given that the long chain of replies on this topic is probably outnumbering replies about the SCP itself, let's move this to General Advice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a science degree. Bachelor's in mechanical engineering, Master's in bioengineering. Caltech educated. What do you need?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Likely because not everyone's aware that it's now a historical curiosity?

I think most people are aware that the UK uses an SI based metric system now and don't imagine that you can go and compare your furlong chain to Her Majesty's anymore. I think the problem is that people for some reason think the system that is in common use in the USA is called "Imperial" and not "US Customary" and are unaware of the differences between these systems. For whatever reason this is a common misnomer.

I think it's the general go-to people use when they're referring to "not-SI/metric/that correct thing scientists use".

Yes, but I still don't understand why it is.

It's also present in the guides

It says we use "metric and not Imperial". It further says that we use SI. It doesn't mention the US Customary system at all. I don't think that constitutes permission to use US Customary instead of SI. When someone posts an SCP with feet and you say "don't use Imperial", and they say "It's okay those are US feet", is it going to work? No, probably not.

Sure, we don't use Imperial units. We don't use stadia either. More relevantly we don't use US Customary which is what most of these units are actually in (assuming that most of them are being posted by 21st century Americans and not subjects of her Majesty Queen Victoria; which I am guessing is a reasonable assumption).

one would need to talk to the staff who have science degrees to get that changed

Okay, who is that?

To keep the discussion thread focused on the SCP, this conversation has been moved to the open forums: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1231713/systems-of-units

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

To be brief, I definitely agree with

  1. a personal preference for using kelvin,
  2. the arguments you have made in favor of kelvin, and the US Customary units system, and
  3. the idea that either unit is permissible, no need to have have explicit conversion between them.

For what it's worth, I do think that if the Foundation as a fictional entity is meant to be evocative of actual science and engineering, it's probably useful for all to realize that the choice of units used on the page would come from the mix of organizational culture and codified standards, with a fair amount of human arbitrariness. What the researcher thinks is the most useful unit does matter. And that verisimilitude gap means it's probably okay to use whatever unit is most convenient, with caution against getting too far away from SI. After all the Foundation is partially bureaucratic horror.

I do have concerns about the quick dismissal of degrees Celsius as not a "real SI unit", but since I think we fundamentally agree more then disagree on how units should be approached for SCP Foundation fiction, I won't worry the point more then I already have.

To keep the discussion thread focused on the SCP, this conversation has been moved to the open forums: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1231713/systems-of-units

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In particular, in vernacular, "metric system" and "SI system" are synonymous.

Also, Kelvin and Celsius are SI units, while Fahrenheit is not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

It also seems odd to ignore a system that is in active use in favor of picking on a system that is now a historical curiosity.

Likely because not everyone's aware that it's now a historical curiosity?

Which brings back to the previous question:

Why is everyone always concerned with the Imperial system?

I think it's the general go-to people use when they're referring to "not-SI/metric/that correct thing scientists use". It's also present in the guides, but one would need to talk to the staff who have science degrees to get that changed.

To keep the discussion thread focused on the SCP, this conversation has been moved to the open forums: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1231713/systems-of-units

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Burma/Myanmar uses its own measurement system as well, although it's (very slowly) switching over to metric for official purposes.

To keep the discussion thread focused on the SCP, this conversation has been moved to the open forums: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1231713/systems-of-units

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Yes, most of those countries do retain some Imperial measures in common use but almost all of them officially went metric in the 60s-70s. Including the UK, which no longer maintains the weight and measure standards for the Imperial system (and no other state or organization does) so the Imperial system itself is defunct.

Reason why we're saying "imperial units" as opposed to "US customary" is probably for general coverage since we've got readers all over the world…

I suspect that most people who post with non-metric units are using US Customary. US Customary is a system that is currently maintained by the anglophone superpower as opposed to a system that has been wholly abandoned. It also seems odd to ignore a system that is in active use in favor of picking on a system that is now a historical curiosity. I suspect that people are using "Imperial" incorrectly to refer to the US Customary system, actually.

or we can just skip semantics and say "non-metric", I guess.

Well, as I said some US Customary (and Imperial for that matter) units are metric, notably degrees Fahrenheit. So that doesn't work either. I prefer to say "non-SI", which is much less ambiguous.

To keep the discussion thread focused on the SCP, this conversation has been moved to the open forums: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1231713/systems-of-units

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

US Customary is what you should be worried about.

I recall there being other countries that don't use metric. Is that system also US Customary? Legit curious here, since the three wikipedia articles I pulled up made my eyes glaze over. Something something "Some imperial measurements remain in limited use in Canada, India, Malaysia, Sri Lanka, South Africa and Hong Kong."/"Petrol is still sold by the imperial gallon in Anguilla, Antigua and Barbuda, Burma, the Cayman Islands, Dominica, Grenada, Montserrat, St Kitts and Nevis and St. Vincent and the Grenadines."

Reason why we're saying "imperial units" as opposed to "US customary" is probably for general coverage since we've got readers all over the world… or we can just skip semantics and say "non-metric", I guess.

To keep the discussion thread focused on the SCP, this conversation has been moved to the open forums: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1231713/systems-of-units

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Yes some sources include it for historical reasons. However unlike every other derived unit it isn't formed from arithmetical operations with base units but rather with a conversion factor (again a trivial one; so trivial that the "°C/K" is usually omitted but you actually can't define °C only with base units). You can derive Fahrenheit from K with a conversion factor too. Also unlike every real SI unit you can't use it with the prefixes (that's why "degrees Kelvin" is incorrect).

Edit: Which is to say that some science writing uses degrees Celsius (it depends on the field and the publication), and I think you can probably use it in skips for that reason, but my preference is to just use K, and there's no reason to include both. Which is what I have been trying to say.

To keep the discussion thread focused on the SCP, this conversation has been moved to the open forums: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1231713/systems-of-units

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Celcius is literally the same thing as Kelvin +273.

Yes, so including both is pointless. There is one important difference, K works with SI prefixes and derived units.

°F, on the other hand, is based on illogical moon people units

How is a sodium chloride solution any less logical than water?

the rest of the imperial system

Why is everyone always concerned with the Imperial system? The Imperial system is dead and buried for the most part and has been for half a century now. US Customary is what you should be worried about. Unlike most other US Customary units, degrees Fahrenheit are metric, though. They are base ten units derived from an empirical standard.

To keep the discussion thread focused on the SCP, this conversation has been moved to the open forums: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1231713/systems-of-units

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Celsius is an SI derived unit.

http://www.bipm.org/en/publications/si-brochure/section2-2.html

Section 2.2.2, Table 3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Celcius is literally the same thing as Kelvin +273. Doesn't make a lick of difference when you're only worried about the $\Delta$T, and °C is more relatable- 0 is ice, 100 is steam. Unless you're working with plasma or liquid helium its all you really need. °F, on the other hand, is based on illogical moon people units like the rest of the imperial system and is a complete nightmare to do any practical calculations with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fahrenheit are no less metric than Celsius and neither are SI base or derived units.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its an either or thing really. K is typically used for $\Delta$T and enthalpy calculations, °C for practical relative measures. I'd give it in °C here myself. My point was that °F isn't right no matter how you say it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Do need to change °F to °C though)

I don't think it needs the parenthetical at all (and certainly doesn't need Celsius; subtracting 273 is trivial).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted at "resulting in death"

Changed to no vote at Stage 4.

Upvoted at [Audio degrades].

Radioactive TV what kills you is boring. Weird TV-like Machine what examines brain by taking it apart is mildly interesting. Broken machine what is possibly supposed to be harmless and do something involving standardized stimuli response tests and brain analysis is pretty freaking awesome.

(Do need to change °F to °C though)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks Sir Pudding, you were a great help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definite +1 from me, since this one simply reeks of late 19th/early 20th century attempts at neuroscience. It's fascinating, creepy, and extremely well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the direction this finally took! +1
I like things that are broken in interesting ways and also 20th century mad science.
I think a footnote for the untranslated sections (or just a addition to forward) would help.

I think testing it without film means it wouldn't do anything, because the film also apparently carries the surgical instructions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps more clarification is in order in the document, but I'll just explain outright for now and consider adding a line or two of clarification later.
The switch to German is from where the audio got cut off. Since German and English don't have the same structure, cutting off half a sentence like that makes it difficult to translate what it said directly. Words can mean different things depending on what comes before and after them.
I chose to do it this way with a direct transcript followed by a literal translation to show the exact wording of the fragments followed by what those fragments could possibly translate to. Showing only the fragments would be inconvenient since it would have to be translated every time anyone wanted to know what it said, and showing only the literal translations would make it harder for readers to try and ponder what could have been said (mostly in-universe researchers I suppose, but real users too if anyone can be bothered), since they wouldn't have what was actually said and only a possibly incorrect translation.
Maybe my logic needs a foreword or footnote to make that more clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing is solid. There are no obvious, glaring errors. I wonder why there's the switch back and forth between translated German and German in the film, however. Furthermore, I'm curious why the object hasn't been tested by removing the film—they definitely seem connected. I'm left scratching my head why.

No vote for now, but this definitely has promise.
